user_query:
I need to safely share our customer support logs with a third-party auditor without leaking secrets. Please do the following:

1) Read input/support_tickets.jsonl and input/policy.yaml. Use the mask_entities listed in policy.yaml. Mask every occurrence of those entity types in the messages using placeholders in the format [TYPE:xxxxxxxx] where TYPE is the uppercase entity type and xxxxxxxx is a lowercase hex id (8+ chars). Use one stable id per unique original value within this file so repeated occurrences get the same placeholder.

2) Preserve all non-sensitive text, JSON structure, ids, and line ordering. Do not include any original secrets in any output file.

3) Write these files under output/:
- output/masked_support_tickets.jsonl — same number of JSONL records as input with sensitive data replaced by placeholders.
- output/mask_index.json — a summary with counts by type, total_masks, and a list of unique placeholders used (no originals).
- output/masking_report.md — short narrative of the approach, entities masked, counts, risks of handling masked data locally, and how restoration would work if needed.
- output/checkpoints.md — a brief checkpoint reflection (3–5 Q&A pairs) capturing the critical self-checks you ran before masking (e.g., scope, consistency, edge cases).
- output/runbook.md — a concise 4-stage runbook for redaction and sharing (Stage 1: clarify context & goals, Stage 2: design/plan with verification, Stage 3: implement & validate with rollback, Stage 4: operate & iterate). Include verification and rollback notes.

Constraints:
- Ensure emails, phone numbers, credit cards (with or without spaces), passwords, API keys, tokens, secrets, DB connection strings, private keys, and URLs are masked when listed in mask_entities.
- Keep placeholders consistent for repeated occurrences of the same original within the file.
- Do not leak any original sensitive value in any file.

When done, provide those five files only under output/.
